meta.title: SLK / SLC Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.tagPill: Mercedes-Benz • SLK / SLC • 1996–2020 • 3 Generations • M271 • M272 • OM651 • M274 • M152
hero.h1: SLK / SLC Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every SLK and SLC generation from the R170 to the R172 — engine codes, known failures, replacement costs, and honest economics. Backed by real UK SLK and SLC engine enquiry data [MB-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The SLK/SLC rule: buy the engine, not the badge — the M271's timing chain is the defining risk; the M272 V6 and OM651 diesel are the safe bets.
M272 V6 (SLK 280/350) — robust, well-understood, fewer catastrophic failures [PATTERN DATA]
M271 (SLK 200 Kompressor) — timing chain stretch and tensioner failure, often catastrophic if ignored [PATTERN DATA]
R172 SLK 250 CDI (2012–2015) with OM651 — diesel economy, 204 bhp, and the cleanest reliability record in the range [PATTERN DATA]
M271 timing chain rattle — cold-start noise is the primary warning sign [PATTERN DATA]
💎 Affordable Roadster — The most accessible two-seat Mercedes

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The SLK/SLC is Mercedes' most accessible roadster — but the M271 engine's timing chain is a £1,500–£2,500 time bomb if you buy the wrong one. Buy the M272 V6 or OM651 diesel, check the service history, and enjoy one of the best value used convertibles on the market.

rows.value: M271 — timing chain and tensioner failures [PATTERN DATA]
M272 V6 / OM651 diesel — both have clean records [PATTERN DATA]
M271 — timing chain stretch, tensioner failure, oil feed issues [PATTERN DATA]

generations.h2: SLK / SLC Generations — Find Your Vehicle
generations.subHeadline: Three generations of Mercedes' compact roadster — from the original "Kompressor" to the final SLC.

cards.title: R170 (1996–2004)
cards.verdict: The SLK that started it all — and the one that's now entering classic territory. The M111 Kompressor engine is robust but ageing; rust is the real enemy on R170s. Buy a well-preserved example for weekend use, not daily driving. Values are starting to climb.

cards.title: R171 (2004–2010)
cards.verdict: The best-looking SLK generation — but the M271 timing chain is the elephant in the room. The M272 V6 (SLK 280/350) is the one to buy: smooth, robust, and without the chain anxiety. Check the service history, listen for cold-start rattle, and buy a V6 if your budget allows.

cards.title: R172 SLK (2011–2015) / SLC (2016–2020)
cards.verdict: The SLK/SLC to buy. The R172 offers the widest engine choice — from the 1.8-litre M271 (carrying the same chain risk as the R171) to the 204 bhp OM651 diesel (the smart money choice), the M274 turbo petrol (cleaner and more modern), and the 5.5-litre M152 V8 in the SLK 55 AMG. The 2016 SLC facelift brought M274 engines and a naming change — but the R172 platform is the same underneath.

cards.title: Comparison sub-block: R171 M271 vs R172 M271 — same engine, same risk

engineDatabase.h2: SLK / SLC Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the Mercedes SLK and SLC — verified against OEM technical data and cross-referenced with documented failure patterns.

commonProblems.subHeadline: Six known failure patterns — drawn from specialist interviews, owner forums, and documented Mercedes-Benz TSBs.

problems.description: The defining SLK/SLC engine problem. The M271's timing chain stretches and the hydraulic tensioner fails, causing a rattle on cold start. If ignored, the chain can skip teeth, bending valves and destroying the engine. Most common on 60k–100k mile examples. £1,500–£2,500 chain replacement; £2,000–£3,800 for a full recon if catastrophic [MB-QUOTE].

The M271's oil feed bolt can fail, causing oil to drain from the cylinder head when the engine is switched off. This results in blue smoke on start-up and, over time, can lead to timing chain tensioner failure due to oil starvation. A £50–£100 bolt replacement kit resolves the issue — but it's often misdiagnosed as a more serious problem.

Not an engine problem, but the single most common reason R170 and early R171 SLKs are scrapped. Rear subframe corrosion is a known issue, with a 20-year extended warranty programme in place in some markets. UK owners should inspect the subframe at every service. £500–£1,500 subframe replacement if caught early.

The vario-roof's hydraulic system can develop leaks, and the microswitches can fail, preventing the roof from opening or closing. Common on higher-mileage examples. £500–£1,500 repair depending on the component.
The OM651 diesel's EGR valve can fail, triggering engine warning lights and reduced performance. Common on urban-driven examples. £400–£800 EGR replacement [MB-QUOTE].

A known software issue affecting the Electronic Stability Program (ESP) on R172 SLK and SLC models. Mercedes issued a recall for 134 vehicles in Austria — UK owners should check if their vehicle is affected. Typically a software update resolves the issue.

marketIntelligence.h2: SLK / SLC Market Intelligence — What UK Owners Told Us

signals.data: High — the defining SLK/SLC problem [PATTERN DATA]
signals.demandTrend: ⬆ Rising — more R171 and early R172 examples reaching the 60k–100k mileage window

signals.data: Moderate — buyers seeking the "safe" engine [PATTERN DATA]
Growing — diesel SLKs are increasingly popular as used buys [PATTERN DATA]
signals.demandTrend: ⬆ Rising — fuel economy and reliability are driving demand

signals.data: Increasing — the original SLK is entering collector territory [THIRD-PARTY]
signals.demandTrend: ⬆ Rising — values are climbing for well-preserved examples

insights: Interesting insight: The M271 timing chain problem is the single most-discussed SLK/SLC issue in the UK owner community — but it's also the most preventable. A £1,500–£2,500 chain replacement at 60k–80k miles is significantly cheaper than a £3,800 engine replacement if the chain fails catastrophically. The smart money buys an R171 or early R172 SLK with the chain already done, or budgets for it upfront.

The M272 V6 (SLK 280/350) is the engine that SLK specialists recommend most often — it's smoother, more powerful, and materially more reliable than the M271. The fuel economy penalty is real (25–28 mpg vs 32–35 mpg for the M271), but the absence of timing chain anxiety makes it the better long-term ownership proposition. The SLK 55 AMG's M152 V8 is in a different league — 416 bhp, 5.5 litres, and a sound that defines the breed — but running costs are high and specialist support is essential.

The R172 diesel (SLK 250 CDI / SLC 250 d) is the dark horse of the range. The OM651 engine delivers 204 bhp, 50+ mpg, and materially fewer failure reports than the M271 petrol. It's not as characterful as the V6 or V8, but for a daily-driven roadster, it's the smartest financial choice. The R172 platform's 2016 SLC facelift brought the M274 turbo petrol — a cleaner, more modern engine than the M271, with no timing chain risk. If you're buying a 2016–2020 SLC, the M274 is the pick.

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every SLK / SLC Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: The M271 engine is the single biggest risk in the SLK/SLC range — cold-start rattle is the warning sign, and a £1,500–£2,500 chain replacement is cheaper than a £3,800 engine. But the smart money buys the M272 V6 (SLK 280/350) or the OM651 diesel (SLK 250 CDI) and avoids the anxiety altogether. Buy the engine, not the badge. MERCEDES-BENZ SLK / SLC — SECTIONS 8-14

replacementCosts.h2: SLK / SLC Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing — used, reconditioned and rebuilt, supply-only, with independent labour guidance.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting an R170 SLK engine typically takes 8–12 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. R172 SLK (2011–2015) / SLC (2016–2020) Variant Engine Code Used (Supply) Reconditioned (Supply) Rebuilt (Supply) Labour Hours SLK 200 (2011–2015) M271 (late) £600–£1,200 £2,000–£3,800 £3,000–£5,000 8–11 hrs SLK 200 (2016–2020 SLC) M274 £800–£1,800 £2,500–£4,500 £3,800–£6,000 8–11 hrs SLK 250 CDI (2012–2015) OM651 £800–£1,800 £2,800–£4,800 £4,000–£6,000 8–11 hrs SLC 250 d (2016–2020) OM651 £800–£1,800 £2,800–£4,800 £4,000–£6,000 8–11 hrs SLK 300 (2012–2015) M276 V6 £1,000–£2,200 £3,000–£5,500 £4,500–£7,500 10–13 hrs SLK 55 AMG (2012–2015) M152 V8 £3,000–£5,500 £6,000–£10,000 £9,000–£14,000 14–18 hrs Labour estimate: Fitting an R172 SLK or SLC engine typically takes 8–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. The SLK/SLC rule — buy the engine, not the badge: A £2,000–£3,800 M271 recon against a £3,000–£6,000 R171 SLK sits at 33–127% of vehicle value — well above our 70% Mercedes threshold on lower-value examples, and a serious risk even on the higher end. The M271's timing chain is the defining risk: a £1,500–£2,500 preventative chain replacement is cheaper than a £3,800 engine if it fails catastrophically. The same M272 V6 recon at £2,500–£4,500 against a £4,000–£8,000 R171 sits at 31–113% — still a significant percentage, but the M272 is materially more reliable and less likely to need replacement in the first place. The R172 diesel (OM651) at £2,800–£4,800 against a £6,000–£12,000 car sits at 23–80% — the safest economics in the range. The rule: Buy an M271 SLK only if the chain has already been replaced, or budget £1,500–£2,500 for a preventative replacement. Buy the M272 V6, OM651 diesel or M274 turbo petrol for the best long-term economics. The SLK 55 AMG is a different proposition entirely — buy for passion, not pound-stretching.

engineEvolution.h2: How the SLK / SLC Engine Range Evolved — 1996 to 2020

eras.whyBmwChanged: The SLK invented the folding hardtop — the M111 supercharged four-cylinder was the volume engine, delivering 163–192 bhp with the characterful whine of a supercharger
eras.worthKnowing: The M111 is robust and well-understood — but ageing R170 chassis rust is the real enemy. The M112 V6 is the one to buy for R170 reliability

eras.era: R171 Era — Design Icon, M271 Risk
eras.whyBmwChanged: The R171 brought sharper styling and a wider engine range — but the M271's timing chain design was a step backward from the M111's reliability
eras.worthKnowing: The M272 V6 (SLK 280/350) is the R171 sweet spot — smooth, 231–272 bhp, and materially more reliable than the M271. The SLK 55 AMG's M113 V8 is thunderous but rare

eras.era: R172 SLK Era — Wide Choice, New Diesel
eras.keyEngines: M271 (continued), M276 (3.5 V6), OM651 (2.1 diesel), M152 (5.5 AMG V8)
eras.whyBmwChanged: The R172 introduced the OM651 diesel to the SLK range for the first time — 204 bhp, 50 mpg, and the cleanest reliability record in the range
eras.worthKnowing: The SLK 250 CDI with OM651 is the smart money choice — the V6 is more characterful, but the diesel's fuel economy and reliability are unmatched in the SLK range

eras.era: SLC Facelift — M274 Arrives, M271 Retired
eras.whyBmwChanged: The 2016 SLC facelift replaced the M271 with the modern M274 turbo petrol — cleaner, more powerful, and without the timing chain risk
eras.worthKnowing: The SLC 200 and 300 with M274 are the best petrol SLK/SLCs ever made — the M271's issues are finally behind us. The SLC was discontinued in 2020 with no direct successor

whoShouldBuy.h2: Who Should Buy an SLK / SLC?

profiles.verdict: The SLK/SLC is the perfect weekend roadster — and the R171 with M272 V6 is the sweet spot. £4,000–£8,000 buys a car that still looks like £40,000, with a robust V6 engine and the character of a naturally-aspirated six-cylinder. Check the roof mechanism, check for rust on the subframe, and enjoy open-top driving without the M271 anxiety.

The R172 SLK 250 CDI or SLC 250 d is the one for daily driving — 204 bhp, 50 mpg, and the OM651's clean reliability record. The R172's folding hardtop makes it a year-round proposition, and the diesel's torque makes it effortless in traffic. Buy a well-maintained example with documented service history and enjoy affordable roadster motoring.

The sub-£3,000 R170 SLK is the most tempting bad decision in the affordable roadster market — but rust on the subframe and ageing electronics are genuine risks. The M111 engine is robust, but the car around it is now 20+ years old. Buy an R170 only if you're comfortable with classic car ownership — or budget £2,000 for a rust-free example with a service history.

The R171 is the best-looking SLK generation — period. The sharp, muscular styling still turns heads, and the interior has held up better than the R170's. Buy the M272 V6 version for the engine that matches the looks — the SLK 350 is the one to have, with 272 bhp and a soundtrack that suits the car's character.

The SLK/SLC is one of the most accessible Mercedes-Benz models to own — and the R172 with M274 or OM651 is the modern choice. £8,000–£14,000 buys a car with modern safety, good fuel economy, and a folding hardtop that works year-round. The SLC 250 d is the one to buy if you want the lowest running costs; the SLC 200 with M274 is the pick if you want petrol refinement.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My SLK / SLC Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: The SLK/SLC's defining economic question is the M271 timing chain: a £1,500–£2,500 preventative repair is cheaper than a £3,800 engine replacement if the chain fails catastrophically. The M272 V6, OM651 diesel and M274 petrol are all materially safer propositions.

trustBlock.h2: Why SLK / SLC Owners Trust MercedesEngines.uk

signals.title: Built on Real Data
signals.text: Every reliability rating and cost range on this page comes from documented failure patterns, specialist interviews, and owner-forum consensus — not manufacturer marketing, not forum guesswork. The SLK/SLC is one of the most-discussed used roadsters in the UK market [PATTERN DATA].

signals.title: We Tell You When Not to Repair
signals.text: Where replacement cost exceeds what the car is worth, we say so. The M271's £2,000–£3,800 recon against a £3,000–£6,000 R171 fails the 70% Mercedes threshold on lower-value examples. A preventative chain replacement at £1,500–£2,500 is the smarter financial move — and we say that explicitly.

signals.title: Vetted Mercedes
signals.text: Benz Specialists — Our network includes 25+ vetted Mercedes-Benz specialists across the UK, each specifically assessed for SLK/SLC work — timing chain assessment, roof mechanism diagnosis, and subframe rust inspection. We don't recommend generalists who happen to take on Mercedes jobs.

signals.title: Part of Engine Finders
signals.text: MercedesEngines.uk is part of the Engine Finders family, the UK's leading engine replacement marketplace — giving you the research depth of an independent authority with a vetted route to action when you need one.

items.question: VIN plate — located on the driver's side door pillar or under the bonnet. The engine code is usually in the 7th and 8th positions of the VIN.
Vehicle documentation — your V5C logbook lists the engine number; the engine code is the first part of that number.

Engine block stamping — the engine code is stamped directly onto the block. On the M271, it's on the front of the engine near the timing cover; on the M272, it's on the top of the block under the plastic cover.
items.answer: Don't rely on the badge: An "SLK 200" badge can mean M111 (R170), M271 (R171 or early R172), or M274 (SLC 200) — the same badge, three different engines. Check the VIN or registration year to confirm. If you're unsure, enter your registration number into our calculator and we'll identify it for you. Use the calculator → Q8: SLK vs SLC — what's the difference — which is more reliable? The SLK (2011–2015) and SLC (2016–2020) are the same car underneath. The name changed in 2016 to align with Mercedes' new naming convention (SUVs = GL, saloons = CL, roadsters = SL, with SLC being the compact roadster). The difference: The SLC facelift brought new engines (M274 replacing M271), a revised front end, and updated interior tech. The SLC is more reliable because it uses the M274 engine — which has no timing chain risk — whereas the pre-2016 SLK used the M271 with its known chain issues. Our verdict: Buy the SLC (2016–2020) for the M274 engine and the facelift improvements. Buy the SLK (2011–2015) only if you find a well-maintained OM651 diesel example — the M271 petrol carries the same risk as the R171. Read the full comparison: SLK vs SLC →
